First, big thanks to Hexschool's principal Liao Wei-Chieh for writing "30 Days to Master SASS Syntax" — I keep coming back to it as I learn SCSS. It's a real godsend.
This series is my attempt to write an SCSS-focused version based on his article.
What are SCSS/SASS? And which one should I learn?
A lot of people pronounce these as "sass" or "soss" (sound similar in Mandarin). And because the names are different, they assume they must be different things — only the pronunciation is similar.
In my interpretation: SCSS is just another
syntax SASS introduced later. "CSS preprocessor" might sound scary, but no need to worry — SCSS/SASS are just extensions on top of CSS.
You don't need to obsess over whether to learn SASS or SCSS, because when you learn SCSS, you've also learned 60-70% of SASS along the way (some syntax differs).
What are the differences? Easier to just look at them side-by-side.
ps. I'm using CodePen for these examples.
SASS version (note: a space after the colon

SASS drops braces and semicolons, relying mostly on indentation. The code is cleaner but the learning curve is steeper compared to SCSS. If you're not super clear on indentation, you'll hit compilation errors and walls.
SCSS is much closer to regular CSS, so it's easier for beginners. So if you ask me which CSS preprocessor to start with, you probably already know the answer from this intro.

After writing SCSS for a while — putting dev efficiency aside — SCSS is a great way to build programming intuition. SCSS feels a lot like writing code, but without much in the way of logical branching.
